narrative: As a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background, your law school application stood out in some ways, like your solid LSAT score of 42, which is above the typical 36.7 average, and your plan to attend full-time like most students. However, the admissions model, which predicts the chances of passing the bar exam on the first try based on profiles like yours, estimated an 80% probability that you would fail it. This high risk of failure is why your application was rejected—the school uses this prediction to prioritize applicants likely to succeed and pass the bar promptly. The model's decision hinged most on your academic decile rankings from school, particularly your Year 3 performance, which placed you in the bottom decile (1 out of 10) compared to the average student's middle-of-the-pack standing around the 5th or 6th decile. This low Year 3 ranking had the strongest pull toward predicting failure, signaling to the model a potential struggle with sustained high-level coursework essential for bar success. Your Year 1 decile, also at the bottom (1.00 versus the average 5.71), reinforced this concern as the second-biggest factor, suggesting early academic challenges that often persist. On a brighter note, your LSAT score pushed meaningfully in the opposite direction, toward predicting you'd pass, as it demonstrates strong analytical skills above the norm. While your undergraduate GPA of 3.00 is slightly below the 3.21 average, it wasn't among the top influences here. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your profile shows weaker early and later school rankings that outweighed your testing strengths, placing you in a higher-risk group. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile rankings, as improving those could dramatically shift future predictions. You might also seek targeted bar prep resources or admissions counseling to address these gaps head-on. With your LSAT as a foundation, targeted improvements could open doors at other schools soon. Keep pushing—many overcome similar hurdles to succeed in law.
